Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Imagine having a single application serving dozens — or even hundreds — of customers, where each company accesses its own data while sharing the same codebase.
This is exactly what Multi-Tenancy is designed to achieve.
Instead of maintaining one application per customer, a multi-tenant architecture allows a single deployment to serve multiple organizations securely and efficiently.
If you’ve ever built ERP systems, CRM platforms, dealer portals, or SaaS products, you’ve probably encountered this architecture.
In this article, we’ll explore how Multi-Tenancy works and how to implement it using .NET Framework, C#, and SQL Server.

What is Multi-Tenancy?
A tenant is an independent customer that uses your application.
Think about Microsoft’s Office 365.

• Company A logs in.

• Company B logs in.

• Company C logs in.
Everyone uses the same application, but no one can see anyone else’s data.
The application knows which customer is logged in and loads the appropriate data.
Internet

┌────────────────────┐
│ ASP.NET Portal │
└────────────────────┘
│
Detect Current Tenant
│

Customer A Customer B

Common Multi-Tenant Strategies
There are three popular approaches.
1. Shared Database
All customers share the same database.
Customers
Orders
InvoicesTenantId
Every table contains a TenantId column.
Example:
Advantages:

• Lower infrastructure cost

• Easier deployment
Disadvantages:

• Larger databases

• Greater risk if filters are forgotten

2. Shared Server, Separate Databases ⭐
This is one of the most common enterprise architectures.
Application
↓
Customer_A_DB
Customer_B_DB
Customer_C_DBEach customer has its own SQL Server database.
Advantages:

• Better isolation

• Easier backup

• Easier restore

• Better security
This is the architecture we’ll implement.

3. Dedicated Infrastructure
Each customer has:

• Its own server

• Its own application

• Its own database
Highest isolation, highest cost.

Identifying the Current Tenant
The first step is determining which customer is making the request.
This can be done using:

• Login information

• JWT Claims

• URL

• Subdomain

• API Key
Example:
customer-a.company.com↓
Tenant = Customer_A
or
portal.company.com?tenant=Customer_A
Tenant Configuration Table
Instead of hardcoding connection strings, create a configuration database.
Example:
When the user logs in:

Login
↓
Tenant
↓
Configuration Database
↓
Connection String
↓
Customer Database
Creating a Connection Factory
A simple approach is to centralize connection creation.
public class ConnectionFactory
{
public SqlConnection Create(string tenant)
{
string connectionString = TenantRepository
.GetConnectionString(tenant); return new SqlConnection(connectionString);
}
}
Now every repository simply requests the correct connection.
using(var conn = factory.Create(currentTenant))
{
// Execute SQL
}No repository needs to know where the database is located.

Storing the Current Tenant
A common practice is storing the tenant during authentication.
Example:
Session["Tenant"] = tenantName;Later:
string tenant = Session["Tenant"].ToString();In Web API:
HttpContext.Current.Items["Tenant"]Or through Dependency Injection.

Repository Example
public List<Customer> GetCustomers(string tenant)
{
using(var conn = factory.Create(tenant))
{
// Query database
}
}The repository never needs to know whether it’s connecting to Customer_A, Customer_B, or Customer_C, it only receives the tenant.

Security Considerations
A secure multi-tenant application should never:
❌ Accept connection strings from the client
❌ Trust user input to determine the database
❌ Cache tenant information globally
Instead:

• Validate authentication first

• Load tenant information from a trusted source

• Keep connection strings encrypted

• Log every tenant access

Performance Tips
As the number of customers grows, consider:

• SQL Connection Pooling

• Memory Cache for tenant configuration

• Asynchronous database access

• Read replicas

• Retry policies

• Distributed caching
These techniques allow the same application to serve hundreds of tenants efficiently.

Advantages
✔ Single codebase
✔ Easier maintenance
✔ Lower deployment cost
✔ High scalability
✔ Customer isolation
✔ Easier onboarding of new clients

Final Thoughts
Multi-Tenancy is one of the most important architectural patterns for SaaS applications.
Whether you’re building ERP systems, dealer portals, or enterprise platforms, separating customer data while keeping a single application can dramatically reduce maintenance costs and improve scalability.
With a well-designed tenant resolution strategy, centralized connection management, and secure authentication, implementing Multi-Tenancy in .NET Framework is both straightforward and highly effective.
The result is a cleaner architecture, happier customers, and a platform ready to grow.
